title: US PPI Forecast Snapshot
summary: May headline PPI surged +1.1% MoM and +6.5% YoY on a gasoline +23.4% spike. Core also surprised hot at +0.8% MoM — the largest advance since March 2022. June energy prices are softening with WTI near $70, but core passthrough from petrochemicals and freight remains sticky. The market will watch whether core goods re-accelerate or begin to fade.
baseCase: Headline cools on energy mean-reversion; core stays sticky near +0.4% on lagged passthrough

release.name: PPI
release.thesis: May headline surged +1.1% on gasoline +23.4%, but core also printed hot at +0.8% MoM. June energy PPI likely cools as WTI fell from $115 to $70. Core goods passthrough remains sticky near +0.4% on lagged petrochemical and freight costs. The Fed watches core; headline volatility is noise.

scenarios.label: Base case
scenarios.outcome: Headline +0.2%, core +0.4%. Energy mean-reverts partially; core goods pressure fades but services remain sticky.
scenarios.marketReadthrough: Rates digest the headline but focus on core. No major Fed repricing. Cyclicals stabilize if margin narrative holds. Treasury yields drift lower.
scenarios.invalidation: Core services re-accelerate above +0.6% MoM or headline surprises above +0.5%.

scenarios.label: Hotter pipeline pressure
scenarios.outcome: Headline +0.6%, core +0.6%. Energy does not cool enough; core goods re-accelerate on tariff passthrough and freight.
scenarios.marketReadthrough: Rates higher, USD firmer, margin-sensitive equities under pressure. Fed hawkishness extends on core stickiness.
scenarios.invalidation: Hot print is driven by narrow commodity volatility with weak pass-through into CPI or margins.

scenarios.label: Cleaner disinflation pulse
scenarios.outcome: Headline -0.1%, core +0.2%. Energy collapses further; core passthrough fades faster than expected.
scenarios.marketReadthrough: Fed-cut odds rise, rates soften, broader equity appetite improves. Margin relief narrative strengthens.
scenarios.invalidation: Market already priced a benign outcome before release or energy snaps back on supply risk.

setups.bias: Long duration if core PPI cools without hawkish spillover
setups.trigger: Core PPI <= +0.3% MoM and revisions are benign
setups.invalidation: Broad upstream re-acceleration with hawkish rates repricing

setups.bias: Constructive only if margin pressure eases from May highs
setups.trigger: Core goods and services pipeline both avoid upside surprise; May core hotness does not repeat
setups.invalidation: Input cost shock restarts (energy spikes above $85 or freight re-accelerates)

setups.bias: Long on hotter PPI surprise with rates follow-through
setups.trigger: Headline and core both beat with higher terminal-rate pricing
setups.invalidation: Rates fade despite the beat or oil collapse drives risk-on

watchItems.label: Final demand services
watchItems.note: May services slowed to +0.3% from +0.7%. A re-acceleration above +0.5% would flip the narrative.

watchItems.label: Commodity complex
watchItems.note: WTI near $70 erased war premium. If it holds, energy PPI cools. Watch supply risk and tariff pass-through.

watchItems.label: Pass-through
watchItems.note: May core +0.8% was well above trend. Focus on whether June confirms cooling or extends the hot streak.

keyDrivers: May core PPI printed +0.8% MoM — the largest advance since March 2022. This is not just an energy story.
Oil war premium evaporated: WTI $115 -> $70. Energy PPI should mean-revert in June, but core passthrough lags.
PPI matters most when it shifts margin expectations or the CPI narrative. June CPI is the transmission confirmation.
Services less trade/transport/warehousing rose +0.7% in May. If this repeats, core stays sticky regardless of energy.
risks: May headline and core were both hot. Revisions can dominate the first market read.
Energy volatility is two-way. Supply shocks or tariff escalation can restart input cost inflation.
Macro tape can be driven by yields or oil even if PPI itself is tame.
Core goods may re-accelerate if petrochemical passthrough and freight embedding continue.
